title: Two-way ANOVA and interactions
intro: One-way ANOVA compares groups by ONE factor. But in life factors come several at a time: the plan and the channel; the drug and the age. Two-way ANOVA takes them apart together — and catches the most interesting thing: the interaction, when the effect of one factor depends on the other.

beats.text: The idea is the same — compare the spread explained by the factors with the noise spread inside. But now there are two factors: A — the button design (control/test, the X axis), B — the platform (two lines: mobile and desktop). ANOVA decomposes the spread into A's contribution, B's contribution, their interaction and the residual noise — with an F for each. On the chart you can see it by the slope and the parallelism of the lines.
The key new concept is the interaction.
beats.predict: Set the "interaction" slider to 0, then increase it. What happens to the two lines?
beats.reveal: At zero the lines are parallel — the test's effect is the same on mobile and desktop (no interaction). As it grows they diverge and may cross: on mobile the test helps, on desktop it hurts. That IS the interaction — factor A's effect depends on B's level. The average effect here is deceptive (remember ATE/CATE and Simpson): an interaction is the statistical name of a heterogeneous effect.

beats.text: How to read the result: three tests. The main effect of A (averaged over B), the main effect of B (averaged over A), and the A×B interaction. If the interaction is significant, the main effects are interpreted with care: saying "the design helps" is meaningless — you must say "helps on iOS, hurts on Android".

The link to what you know. Two-way ANOVA is again a special case of regression: y on two categorical factors plus their product (the interaction term). And the "interaction" here is exactly the CATE/heterogeneity from A/B testing, just in variance packaging.

Why an analyst cares: real decisions are almost always multi-factor, and the interactions are the most valuable part. They are what says "who exactly the change suits", not "does it work on average". Averaging over an interaction is a frequent way to make a harmful decision.

definitions.text: analysis of two factors at once: their main effects and the interaction.
the effect of one factor averaged over the levels of the other.
one factor's effect depends on the other's level; the statistical name of a heterogeneous effect.
two-way ANOVA = a regression on two categorical factors and their product.

practice: With several factors, don't run a pile of separate tests — two-way ANOVA (or a regression with an interaction) takes them apart jointly and honestly catches where the effect depends on the conditions.
A significant interaction is a stop sign for the phrase "helps on average": from there the decision is made by subgroup, not by the overall mean. A direct bridge to segment analysis in A/B.
realLife: Agronomy (ANOVA's birthplace): the yield depends on the cultivar, the fertilizer, and their combination — the interaction shows which cultivar suits which fertilizer.
Product and medicine: a feature's/drug's effect often depends on the segment/patient. The interaction IS the "who exactly it suits" — the most important part of the conclusion.
assumptions: Same as one-way: roughly normal residuals, similar spread in the cells, independence. Plus you need enough observations in EVERY cell (combination of levels), otherwise the interaction is estimated very noisily.
Many factors and levels → many comparisons → the multiplicity risk again; a significant interaction is best confirmed on new data.
